original_abstract:
Selecting appropriate stimuli to induce specific emotional states is a fundamental requirement in affective research. To date, only a few standardized databases offer high-quality auditory, linguistic, or visual materials. While existing repositories have been instrumental in behavioral and neuroimaging studies, researchers have increasingly noted limitations, such as insufficient stimuli in certain subcategories and inconsistent image quality. 

In this article, we introduce the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS), a novel database comprising 1,356 realistic, high-quality photographs categorized into five domains: people, faces, animals, objects, and landscapes. We collected normative affective ratings from 204 participants across three dimensions—valence, arousal, and approach-avoidance—using computerized bipolar semantic slider scales. We present the descriptive statistics for these categories and validate our findings through direct comparisons with the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) and the International Affective Picture System (IAPS). Furthermore, we provide detailed technical parameters for each image, including luminance, contrast, and entropy. By offering a diverse range of validated visual materials alongside physical image properties, NAPS enables researchers to more precisely tailor stimuli to their specific experimental questions. The system is freely available to the scientific community for noncommercial use at http://naps.nencki.gov.pl.
humanized_abstract:
As a preliminary step in most affective research, it is often necessary to select relevant stimuli to elicit the target emotions. Unfortunately, there are only a few databases offering high quality sets of auditory, linguistic or visual materials. Although some of them have been largely employed in behavioral and neuroimaging studies, researchers now consider some of their limitations such as: lack of examples in some affective subcategories and low image quality.

This paper presents a new database of affective images—the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS). It includes 1356 realistic photos in the form of high quality pictures divided into five categories: people, faces, animals, objects and landscapes. The affective values were obtained from 204 participants, in terms of three affective dimensions (valence, arousal and approach-avoidance) using the computer version of bipolar semantic slider scales. The statistics describing particular categories of images obtained in this way were verified against the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) and International Affective Picture System (IAPS) databases. In addition, we present physical parameters of all the images (i.e. luminance, contrast and entropy values). We provide a set of a large number of validated images for a wide variety of experimental designs as well as physical parameters of these images, allowing researchers to select images more exactly fitted to their needs. NAPS is available for noncommercial use at the following web site address: http://naps.nencki.gov.pl.

Unfortunately, there are only a few databases offering high quality sets of auditory, linguistic or visual materials. Although some of them have been largely employed in behavioral and neuroimaging studies, researchers now consider some of their limitations such as: lack of examples in some affective subcategories and low image quality.

This paper presents a new database of affective images—the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS). We provide a set of a large number of validated images for a wide variety of experimental designs as well as physical parameters of these images, allowing researchers to select images more exactly fitted to their needs. NAPS is available for noncommercial use at the following web site address: http://naps.nencki.gov.pl.
